pages.text: High performance concrete with Czech metakaolin: Experimental analysis of strength, toughness and durability characteristics Eva Vejmelková a, Milena Pavlíková a, Martin Keppert a, Zbyneˇ k Keršner b, Pavla Rovnaníková c, Michal Ondrácˇ ek d, Martin Sedlmajer d, Robert Cˇ erny´ a,* a Department of Materials Engineering and Chemistry, Faculty of Civil Engineering, Czech Technical University in Prague, Thákurova 7, 166 29 Prague 6, Czech Republic b Institute of Structural Mechanics, Faculty of Civil Engineering, Brno University of Technology, Vever ˇ í 95, 602 00 Brno, Czech Republic c Institute of Chemistry, Faculty of Civil Engineering, Brno University of Technology, Z ˇ izˇ kova 17, 602 00 Brno, Czech Republic d Institute of Technology of Building Materials and Components, Faculty of Civil Engineering, Brno University of Technology, Vever ˇ í 95, 602 00 Brno, Czech Republic article info Article history: Received 20 August 2009 Received in revised form 12 January 2010 Accepted 15 January 2010 Keywords: High performance concrete Metakaolin Compressive strength Fracture toughness Durability abstract Although the capability of metakaolin as pozzolanic material to improve mechanical and durability prop- erties of concrete if used as partial replacement of Portland cement is well noted in concrete science, its utilization in building industry was limited to date, mainly due to its high price dictated by the low pro- duction amounts. However, with the current shortage of silica fume and high-quality slag in some coun- tries the attitude of concrete producers to metakaolin may change in the near future. This change of mind can be facilitated by providing a more comprehensive view of the properties of composite materials with metakaolin, thus contributing to wider realization of the beneﬁts of metakaolin in concrete. In this paper, an extensive set of parameters of high performance concrete (HPC) with metakaolin including basic phys- ical characteristics, mechanical and fracture-mechanical properties, durability characteristics, hydric and thermal properties and chloride binding characteristics is measured. The experimental results show that the replacement of Portland cement by 10% of metakaolin as an optimal amount leads in most cases either to improvements or at least does not signiﬁcantly impair substantial properties of the analyzed HPC. Basic physical properties and heat transport and storage properties are very similar to common HPC, mechanical and fracture-mechanical properties after 28 days are slightly worse but later improved, water- and water vapor transport parameters are substantially reduced, frost resistance is better, resis- tance against de-icing salts is slightly worse but still meets very well the required criteria. The chemical resistance of concrete with 10% of metakaolin instead of Portland cement in distilled water and HCl is found better than for Portland cement concrete, in MgCl 2 it is slightly worse, and in NH 4Cl, Na 2SO4 and CO 2 almost the same, carbonation is reduced, and chloride binding capacity is increased. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Cement-based composites together with clinker-free compos- ites belong to the most advanced materials used in building prac- tice. They include various speciﬁc material types such as high strength concrete (HSC), high and ultra high performance concrete (HPC, UHPC), reactive powder concrete (RPC), alkali activated alu- minosilicates, etc. An exac

researchers and industry turned their attention towards other sup- plementary binders. Countries with signiﬁcant ferrous metallurgy found a good silica fume replacement in ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBFS). However, the sources of GGBFS are not unlimited. Even in some iron producing countries, there is already shortage of high-quality slag which is necessary for concrete production. In Czech Republic, since 2008 no GGBFS is available on the free market. Cement producers make use of all GGBFS produced in the country and still complain of its lack. Silica fume due to its high price became an exclusive product in concrete industry already years ago, and its use is limited now to the most demanding (and expensive) concrete structures. On the other hand, the coun- try has considerable resources of kaolinic clays which were in the past centuries widely used in porcelain industry. Therefore, metakaolin seems to be a logical substitute for silica fume or GGBFS as alternative silicate binder in concrete production in Czech Republic. Properties of paste, mortar and concrete produced using metakaolin as partial replacement of Portland cement were widely studied by a variety of researchers in the past two decades. Both normal- and high performance concrete mixes were analyzed. The mechanical properties of metakaolin concrete were in the cen- ter of interest of most investigators. In quite a few studies, the opti- mization of the amount of metakaolin used as Portland cement replacement (mainly from the point of view of improvement of compressive strength) was a substantial part of research work. The results showed that while for normal concrete (typically with w/c /C25 0.5 and f c /C25 40–50 MPa) the optimal amount of metakaolin was /C25 20% [3–7], for high performance concrete (typically with w/c /C25 0.3 and fc /C25 80–100 MPa) the best results were achieved with /C25 10% of metakaolin [8–13]. However, the use of metakaolin in Portland cement-based composites also contributed to the enhancement of many other substantial properties. The reﬁnement of pore structure reported in [8,14–16] and decrease in total poros- ity found in [8,15,16] were the fundamental features leading to the improvement of other principal parameters. Among them, the most important were the notable decrease of chloride penetration [11,13,17–20] and water sorptivity [21–23], as well as the in- creased resistance to sulfate attack [5,24]. The reduction of autoge- neous shrinkage [25] and drying shrinkage [26], and reduction of the risk of alkali–silica reaction [27–29] were other positive conse- quences of using metakaolin in concrete. Despite the clear consensus in worldwide research community that metakaolin can be effectively used as partial Portland cement replacement (not only for the beneﬁts to the environment but also for substantial improvements of concrete properties) its utilization in building industry is still far behind ﬂy ash and GGBFS. One of the reasons is that metakaolin is more expensive than Portland cement although its processing involves lower temperatures and its pro- duction cost is signiﬁcantly lower. This was already pointed out years ago [30] but only a little has been changed until now. The production of metakaolin is still much lower compared to Portland cement which drives the price up. However, taking into account the recent problems with silica fume and GGBFS in some countries, the attitude of concrete producers to metakaolin may change

bending strength was determined using the procedure described in C ˇ SN EN 12390- 5 [34], with the loading rate of 0.04 MPa/s. Three 100 /C2 100 /C2 400 mm specimens were used in the test. The experiments were performed after 28 days of standard curing. The details of the speciﬁc tests where mechanical properties were used as criteria for durability assessment are given in Section 3.3. The effective fracture toughness was measured using the effective crack model [35] which combines the linear elastic fracture mechanics and crack length ap- proaches. A three-point bending test of a specimen having a central edge notch with a depth of about 1/3 of the depth of the specimen was used in the experiment (Fig. 2 ). The loaded span was equal to 300 mm. A continuous record of the load– deﬂection ( F–d) diagram was used for the calculation of effective fracture tough- ness. An estimate of fracture energy was obtained from the F–d diagram according to the RILEM method (work-of-fracture). Three 100 /C2 100 /C2 400 mm specimens were used in the fracture-mechanical tests. 3.3. Durability tests Frost resistance tests were carried out according to C ˇ SN 73 1322/Z1:1968 [36]. Frost resistance coefﬁcient K was determined as the ratio of bending or compressive strength of specimens subjected to 100 freezing and thawing cycles to the strength of reference specimens which did not undergo the frost resistance test. Three 100 /C2 100 /C2 400 mm specimens were used. The resistance of studied concrete against de-icing salts was measured accord- ing to Cˇ SN 731326/Z1:1984 [37]. The tested specimens were saturated by water and put into a bath with 3% NaCl solution. Then, freeze/thaw cycles were applied. The test was ﬁnished either after the prescribed number of cycles or after the mass loss exceeded 1000 g/m 2. Three 100 /C2 100 /C2 400 mm specimens were used. The chemical resistance in various environments was tested according to the procedure developed at the Brno University of Technology. The specimens were prepared in 100 /C2 100 /C2 400 mm molds and placed into a climatic chamber with 100% relative humidity environment. After 24 h they were de-molded and stored in the same environment for another 27 days. Then, the specimens were cut to 100 /C2 100 /C2 50 mm blocks and put in groups of three into the environments spec- iﬁed in Table 4 . Test of concrete carbonation was performed in a desiccator where the CO 2 concentration was kept at 65 ± 5 vol.% (the concentration was measured by an IR probe). The carbonation took place in an environment above saturated KNO 3 solution (85 ± 5% relative humidity). The specimens denoted as ‘‘air” in Table 1 were stored in common laboratory conditions at 21 ± 2 /C176C and 45 ± 5% relative humidity; those marked ‘‘distilled water” were in distilled-water bath which was replaced every 10 days. The duration of the chemical resistance test was 60 days. Then, the specimens were subjected to the compressive-strength test. The coefﬁcient of chemical resistance K cr was determined as the ratio of the compressive strength after 60 days in a particular environment and compressive strength after 60 days in laboratory conditions. All the specimens were water-leached after the compres- sive-strength test and the pH value was determined by potentiometry. XRD analysis in the range of H-angle of 5–80/C176was done as well to test the possible appearance of new phases. The specimens exposed to the CO 2 action were after the compressive- stre

can get in direct contact with every small grain of hydrated cement. The modiﬁca- tion used in this paper consisted in using the specimens of more realistic dimen- sions (two 40 /C2 40 /C2 10 mm specimens) instead of crushed ones, as it was proposed in [40]. 4. Experimental results and discussion 4.1. Basic material characteristics The XRD analysis of the composition of the metakaolin concrete mix BM conﬁrmed that after 28 days a part of the pozzolanic reac- tion was already completed; the amount of Ca(OH) 2 was in BM lower than in the reference mix BR ( Figs. 3 and 4 , the lowest curves). In the slump test, BM achieved 130 mm, which was 20 mm less than BR, but the workability was still satisfactory. The results (similarly as for instance in [12]) reﬂected the high spe- ciﬁc surface of metakaolin. The bulk density and matrix density of BM measured by water vacuum saturation method (Table 5 ) were very similar to the ref- erence mix BR, the differences lower than 1% being within the error range of the method. The open porosity was for BM about 5% high- er than for BR. The total pore volume of the paste measured by mercury intrusion porosimetry (coarse aggregates were removed from the specimens) was for BM approximately 6% lower than for BR ( Table 6 ). This was in accordance with similar measure- ments in [8,15,16]. The total pore surface was for BM slightly high- er than for BR ( Table 6) but the overall reﬁnement of pore structure which was reported for instance in [8,14–16] was not conﬁrmed. Fig. 5 shows that in the range of 100 nm–1 lm the volume of pores was in BM higher than in BR, for 10–100 nm an opposite result was obtained. This discrepancy may be caused by the different way of specimen preparation; the specimens in this paper were cut from concrete blocks while in the mentioned references paste speci- mens were prepared. 4.2. Mechanical and fracture-mechanical properties The strength improvement as a consequence of using metakao- lin as partial Portland cement replacement (which was reported for instance in [8–13]) was not conﬁrmed in the measurements in this paper. In the standard 28-days compressive-strength test with cu- bic specimens the material BM with metakaolin showed /C25 10% lower values than the reference mix BR (Table 7 ). Also its bending strength was lower, by /C25 8%. However, in the compression test on fragments after the bending test both BM and BR showed almost identical values which were about 10% higher than for BR on cubic specimens. The size effect was probably responsible for this in- crease in measured strength; the area of specimens was larger Fig. 3. X-ray diffraction analysis of BR specimen. 1 – air, 2 – CO 2, 3 – distilled water, 4 – HCl, 5 – MgCl 2,6–N a 2SO4,7–N H 4Cl. Fig. 4. (approximately 200 /C2 100 mm) than the actual loading area of 100 /C2 100 mm. The 28-days values of all three investigated fracture-mechanical parameters, i.e., effective fracture toughness, effective toughness and speciﬁc fracture energy were for the metakaolin concrete BM signiﬁcantly worse than for the reference concrete BR (Table 8). However, quite an opposite situation was observed after 90 days. While up to 30% decrease from the 28-days values was observed for the reference mix, BM exhibited 10–20% increase of all frac- ture-mechanical parameters. In addition, both toughness parame- ters were for BM after 90 days 10–20% higher than for BR. This is a very good result indicating the signiﬁcance of the pozzolanic reaction in metakaolin concrete for increasing long-term frac- ture-mechanical parameters which was not reported yet. 4.3. Durability tests The frost resistance of the material BM containing metakaolin was excellent; 100 freeze–thaw cycles had no effect on both com- pressive and bending strength (Table 9 ). The performance of the reference BR mix was worse for bending strength but still sufﬁcient for the material to be classiﬁed as frost resistant (the limiting value of the frost resistance coefﬁcient K according to [36] is 0.75). The resistance of both BM and BR against de-icing salts (Fig. 6) was very good (for BR slightly better than for BM), well below the limit of 1000 g m /C0 2 after 100 cycles deﬁned in [37] as satisfactory. The chemical resistance of BM ( Table 10) was in distilled water and HCl better than for BR, in MgCl 2 worse, and in NH 4Cl, Na 2SO4 and CO 2 almost the same but the differences were in all cases rel- atively low. Adopting for chemical resistance a similar criterion for satisfactory performance as for frost resistance, i.e., Kcr > 0.75, it can be concluded that both materials exhibited good chemical resistance in all chosen environments. The improved resistance of metakaolin concrete to acid attacks was found e.g. in [13] for sulfuric acid solution, a good resistance to sulfates in [5,24]. The analysis of water leaches of specimens submerged for 60 days in the environments listed in Table 4 showed that for BM the alkalinity of all leaches (except for CO 2) decreased as compared to the reference mix BR; the pH values were 0.2–0.4 lower (Ta- ble 11 ). As already pointed out in [27–29], this may mean a de- creased risk of expansive alkali–silica reaction. On the other hand, the decrease of pH values was not so high that it could harm the required steel passivation; these were still safely above the generally recognized limiting values [41,42], similarly as it was found for water and chloride solution in [27]. The carbonation ex- tent manifested by the decrease of pH after 60 days in CO 2-rich environment was in BM substantially lower than in the reference mix BR, with pH reduction of only /C25 0.1 against /C25 0.5 in BR. This ﬁnding is in an agreement with measurements of carbonation depth in [21] but in a disagreement with [13]. The results of XRD analysis of specimens submerged for 60 days in selected environments including distilled water, salt solutions and acid ( Table 4 ) did not show any new phases in signiﬁcant amounts for both BM and BR ( Figs. 3 and 4 ). The concentration of possible new phases was in all cases under the sensitivity limit of the analysis. This meant that both materials were very compact; Table 8 Fracture mechanical properties. Material Effective fracture toughness (MPa m 1

good agreement with the measurements reported by other investi- gators in [21–23]. However, neither in this case the pore size distri- bution ( Fig. 5 ) could give a sound explanation so that the most probable reason for the obtained data might be randomly distrib- uted cracks acting as preferential paths for liquid water transport. The hygric strain of BM (Fig. 7 ) was /C25 10–20% higher than for BR but its maximum values of about 5 /C2 10/C0 4 were very low so that they should not present any problems in a construction. The con- cave character of eu (u) curves indicated the higher importance of hygric expansion for lower moisture content, i.e., in the hygro- scopic moisture range. Similar behavior was observed for Portland cement mortar in [43]. 4.5. Thermal properties The thermal conductivity of BM (Fig. 8 ) was in the whole mois- ture range slightly higher in comparison with the reference con- crete BR but the difference was not signiﬁcant, taking into account the measuring uncertainty of the method of ±5%. Similar results were obtained for the speciﬁc heat capacity (Fig. 9 ) where the error range of the experimental method was ±10%. The differ- ences in thermal diffusivity ( Fig. 10 ) were also very low, up to 10%, and reﬂected the differences in thermal conductivity and spe- ciﬁc heat capacity. The obtained data are in basic agreement with previous measurements of thermal properties of HPC [44–46]. 4.6. Chloride binding The metakaolin concrete BM exhibited up to 30% higher chlo- ride binding capacity than the reference HPC mix BR (Fig. 11 ). This can be partially attributed to the long time necessary to perform the measurement which was approximately 6 months; after that time the pozzolanic reaction was probably completed. The results are in a qualitative agreement with the ﬁndings in [27] where higher capacity of concrete with metakaolin to bind chloride ions was reported. 5. Conclusions The measurements of a wide set of properties of high perfor- mance concrete containing 10% of Czech metakaolin as substitute of Portland cement conﬁrmed that metakaolin could effectively serve as an alternative to silica fume and ground granulated blast furnace slag in the countries where these are either not available or economically inconvenient. The experimental results showed that in most cases the properties of metakaolin concrete (MC) were either improved or at least not signiﬁcantly impaired in a compar- ison with common Portland cement high performance concrete (PC). The main results can be summarized as follows: /C15 The basic physical properties of MC were very similar to PC. The bulk density and matrix density were within the error range of the measuring method, the changes in total open porosity were not quite distinct; while water vacuum saturation method gave for MC slightly higher values, mercury intrusion porosimetry provided slightly lower values. /C15 Contrary to some other investigations, the reﬁnement of pore structure was for MC not conﬁrmed but both water and water vapor transport were found to be substantially reduced as com- pared to PC. This could be explained by a more complex topol- ogy of the pore space in MC. /C15 The compressive and bending strength of MC after 28 days were slightly worse but it could be anticipated that the secondary pozzolanic reaction would lead to improvements in long-term view. /C15 The effective fracture toughness, effective toughness and spe- ciﬁc fracture energy were after 28 days for MC wors

the chemical substances could react just on the surface and could not penetrate deeper into the porous structure. Metakaolin is a pozzolanic material which reacts with portlan- dite in hydrated cement, thus decreases its concentration. The low- er magnitude of the portlandite peak in BM was apparent for all environments ( Figs. 3 and 4 ). The lower amount of portlandite in BM as compared with BR was also manifested in the decrease of pH value. In the hydrated cement in concrete, portlandite is the most often chemically attacked phase. With Mg 2+ ions it forms brucite, Mg(OH) 2, which is less soluble than portlandite and cal- cium salts. If SO 2/C0 4 ions are present, gypsum CaSO 4/C12H 2O is pro- duced. It can further react with the aluminate phase of hydrated cement, thus produce ettringite 3CaO /C1Al 2O3/C13CaSO 4/C131H 2O. The reaction of portlandite with HCl leads to neutralization resulting in the production of water and soluble salts. Ammonium salts are decomposed in the presence of portlandite, with NH 3 and cal- cium salts being formed. CO 2 reacts with portlandite in a signiﬁ- cant way; this leads to the decrease of pH of the leachate. 4.4. Hydric properties The water vapor diffusion coefﬁcient of the metakaolin concrete BM was in both wet-cup and dry-cup arrangements substantially lower than for the reference mix BR (Table 12 ). This is in basic accordance with its lower total porosity (Table 6 ) but the differ- ence is too high to be explained in this simple way. As not even the pore distribution (Fig. 5 ) can help to resolve the matter, the more complex topology of the pore space in BM seems to give the required explanation. Water transport was found to be substantially reduced in BM as compared to the reference concrete BR which was manifested in /C25 30% decrease of water absorption coefﬁcient and more than 40% decrease of apparent moisture diffusivity (Table 13 ).
